content.text: In JSX, everything you place between a component's opening and closing tags becomes the value of a built-in prop called
. React bundles that content — whether it is text, elements, or an array — and passes it to the component function just like any other prop.
as part of its props object. It can then render that content wherever it chooses — or ignore it entirely.

A component accesses children the same way it accesses any other prop — either from the props object or through destructuring.

You can pass UI content through an explicitly named prop (
The content is the main purpose of the component
You need multiple independent content slots (header, body, footer)
Callers should be able to write JSX naturally (nested tags)
The content is optional or conditional by design
The component wraps or decorates content (layout, modal, tooltip)
The prop name makes the slot's role explicit (e.g. headerContent)

A component can choose whether and how to render its children. Common patterns include guarding against undefined children, wrapping them in a scroll container, or rendering them only when a condition is true.

APIs have limitations with deep nesting and Fragments, and modern design patterns often avoid them in favour of explicit named slots or context. But for simple cases like counting, iterating, or injecting a shared prop into direct children, they work reliably.

Render Props: children as a Function
is just a prop, it can be a function. The pattern is called a
function and passes data to it, giving the caller control over rendering while the component handles the logic.
Render props were the dominant logic-sharing pattern before hooks. Custom hooks have largely replaced them for pure logic sharing, but render props remain useful for inversion of control over
These patterns all follow the same principle: a component owns the logic and context;
provides the content. The component remains reusable because it does not import or know about any specific child.

children and Context: Combining Both Patterns
prop and React Context work extremely well together. Context distributes shared data
exist at all. A Provider wraps its children with a context value; any component nested inside — no matter how deep — can read that value.
This pattern lets you pass data deep into a tree without prop drilling (
never appears as an explicit prop on
), while still using JSX nesting to express the component tree structure.
Some libraries (Radix UI, Headless UI) use a Slot pattern where a component inspects its

and merges its own behaviour into the child element instead of wrapping it in an extra DOM node. This is useful for accessibility components like tooltips or popovers that need to attach event handlers to the user's own trigger element.
This is an advanced technique. For most components, simply rendering
before being passed to the component. This means that when a wrapping component re-renders, its children elements are new React element
do not necessarily re-render unless their own props changed.
In practice, this rarely causes noticeable performance problems. But understanding that children is a new reference on each parent render explains why
on a wrapper component does not prevent re-renders when the parent re-renders with different children.
Testing children-based components with React Testing Library is straightforward — pass children in JSX just as any user would.

children enables composition: the component owns context and structure; the caller owns content.
